This is with Automa 1.1.0. This is consistent, and occur when executing the 'tokenizer' test when running the test suite. Manually running the tokenizer test code by `include`'ing the file does not trigger a segfault.

The segfault also disappears if I outcomment some tests before the 'tokenizer' tests - in particular, it needs to run both the `test09` and  `tokenizer` to segfault, though the order doesn't matter.

In particular, it seems to be the line
```
Vector(tokens)
```
that triggers the segfault, where `tokens isa Vector{Automa.RegExp.RE}`

following code fails on the e0656ac017a427f739f985809017a33c269b3af1

```julia
julia> collect(Iterators.flatten((1, )))
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching promote_typejoin(::Type{Int64})
The function `promote_typejoin` exists, but no method is defined for this combination of argument types.

Closest candidates are:
  promote_typejoin(::Any, ::Any)
   @ Base promotion.jl:174

Stacktrace:
 [1] eltype(::Type{Base.Iterators.Flatten{Tuple{Int64}}})
   @ Base.Iterators ./iterators.jl:1205
 [2] eltype(x::Base.Iterators.Flatten{Tuple{Int64}})
   @ Base ./abstractarray.jl:241
 [3] _collect(cont::UnitRange{Int64}, itr::Base.Iterators.Flatten{Tuple{Int64}}, ::Base.HasEltype, isz::Base.HasLength)
   @ Base ./array.jl:733
 [4] collect(itr::Base.Iterators.Flatten{Tuple{Int64}})
   @ Base ./array.jl:727
 [5] top-level scope
   @ REPL[2]:1

julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.12.0-DEV.1734
Commit e0656ac017 (2024-12-08 21:48 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (arm64-apple-darwin24.1.0)
  CPU: 11 × Apple M3 Pro
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LLVM: libLLVM-18.1.7 (ORCJIT, apple-m3)
Threads: 1 default, 0 interactive, 1 GC (on 5 virtual cores)
```

Works fine in `1.10.7` and `1.11.2`

```julia
julia> collect(Iterators.flatten((1, ))) 
1-element Vector{Int64}:
 1

julia> versioninfo() # also works on 1.10.7
Julia Version 1.11.2
Commit 5e9a32e7af2 (2024-12-01 20:02 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (arm64-apple-darwin24.0.0)
  CPU: 11 × Apple M3 Pro
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LLVM: libLLVM-16.0.6 (ORCJIT, apple-m2)
Threads: 1 default, 0 interactive, 1 GC (on 5 virtual cores)
```

Interestingly enough, this works fine
```julia
julia> collect(Iterators.flatten((1, (1, ))))
2-element Vector{Int64}:
 1
 1
```
and also this
```julia
julia> collect(Iterators.flatten((1, (1, ), 1)))
3-element Vector{Int64}:
 1
 1
 1
```
but not this
```julia
julia> collect(Iterators.flatten((1, 1, 1)))
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching promote_typejoin(::Type{Int64}, ::Type{Int64}, ::Type{Int64})
The function `promote_typejoin` exists, but no method is defined for this combination of argument types.

Closest candidates are:
  promote_typejoin(::Any, ::Any)
```

So yeah the idea in the original code that the arguments might or might just number or other collections, previously it was possible to mix those. 

More examples of seemingly broken behaviour

julia> a = 1
1

julia> collect(Iterators.flatten((a, )))

All of the above works fine in `1.11.2`.

Initially caught [here](https://github.com/ReactiveBayes/GraphPPL.jl/actions/runs/12234711294/job/34124468216?pr=251) with the following stacktrace 

```julia

am puzzled on why `eltype` does not infer more precisely for `Iterators.map(f, iterators...)`, especially when both the `eltype` of the input iterators and the return type of `f` seem to be inferable.
Would it be possible to improve the `eltype` inference for `Iterators`.

```julia
julia> fun(x) = x^2
fun (generic function with 1 method)

julia> a = Iterators.map(fun, 1:3)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, typeof(fun)}(fun, 1:3)

julia> eltype(a)
Any    # Expected: Int
```

This enhancement could help improve type stability and make `Iterators.map` more consistent with the inferred types of other Julia constructs.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56891/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56891/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2559854845","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56891#issuecomment-2559854845","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56891","id":2559854845,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6YlEj9","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-23T14:57:34Z","updated_at":"2024-12-23T14:57:34Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The issue may be that the purpose of `eltype` is not clearly explained. The purpose of `eltype` is to provide a way to get the element type at compile time, in a manner which does _not_ rely on Julia's own type inference.
This is needed sometimes, because Julia's type inference is considered an optimization, and should not affect the result of any computation (only its efficiency). This, in turn, allows Julia to tweak inference for different use cases without it causing breakage.
Therefore, most code isn't actually allowed to "ask inference" for the known result of some function.

On the other hand, this also implies that users should, where possible, _not_ rely on `eltype`, unless they know that `eltype` will provide a correct, concrete type.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2559854845/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

`julia 1.9.0-beta2`, I'm observing the following behaviour when using `Iterators.flatten` to concatenate two iterators of integers:
```
julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))
Base.Iterators.Flatten{Tuple{Tuple{Int64}, Vector{Int64}}}(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> eltype(iter)
Any
```
It feels like this could be improved? If all the concatenated iterators have `eltype` of `Int64`, then shouldn't `iter` too?

I'm happy to make a PR for this if I'm not missing something and this indeed could be impoved. Version info:

is [defined as](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/b0ac45f3fbc8501c8b3909e838103c88114b29b6/base/regex.jl#L189-L191)
```jl
struct RegexMatch <: AbstractMatch
    match::SubString{String}
    captures::Vector{Union{Nothing,SubString{String}}}
    offset::Int
    offsets::Vector{Int}
    regex::Regex
end
```
which *requires* the `match` to be a `SubString{String}`

I encountered this in JuliaStrings/StringViews.jl#14 — it means that, even though PCRE regex searching is implemented for a `StringView`, the returned `RegexMatch` must *duplicate* the match string into a `String`.  This sucks and will require me to re-implement my own `RegexMatch` with a 1-line change.

Ideally, this would be defined as:
```jl
struct RegexMatch{T} <: AbstractMatch
    match::SubString{T}
    captures::Vector{Union{Nothing,SubString{T}}}
    offset::Int
    offsets::Vector{Int}
    regex::Regex
end
```
instead, to support a wider range of string types. But I guess that would be a breaking change, so it would have to wait for Julia        v=UInt8[0x41 for i=1:801];
       popfirst!(v)
       vv=reshape(v, (800, 1))

       s = String(vvv)
       Ref(s)
       end
foo (generic function with 1 method)

julia> foo()
Base.RefValue{String}(UInt8[0x41, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41  …  0x41, 0x41, 0x00, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41, 0x41])
```
From discourse https://discourse.julialang.org/t/does-julia-have-efficient-move-semantics/122165/33

I think the issue is that `jl_genericmemory_to_string` labors under the mistaken assumption that only a string can be the owner of the memory if `how == 3`.

As a further issue, I am quite unhappy with `jl_genericmemory_to_string` mutating the Memory's `length` field: Memory is supposed to have constant length. If another array, especially a non-Vector, uses the same memory, then it's not supposed to have its size changed under it!

+"""
+    append!(c::Channel, iter)
+
+Append all items in `iter` to the channel `c`. If the channel is buffered, this operation requires
+fewer `lock` operations than individual `put!`s. Blocks if the channel is full.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c)
+1
+

+2
+
+julia> take!(c)
+3
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function append!(c::Channel, iter::T) where {T}
+    if hasmethod(length, Tuple{T})
+        len = length(iter)
+        # shortcircuit for small short objects
+        if len == 0
+            return c
+        elseif len == 1
+            put!(c, @inbounds vec[begin])
+            return c
+        end
+    end
+
+    return isbuffered(c) ? append_buffered(c, iter) : append_unbuffered(c, iter)
+end
+function append!(c1::Channel, c2::Channel{T}) where {T}
+    buff_len = max(c1.sz_max, c2.sz_max)
+    if buff_len == 0
+        return append_unbuffered(c1, c2)
+    end
+
+    buff = Vector{T}(undef, buff_len)
+    while isopen(c2) || isready(c2)
+        take!(c2, buff_len, buff)
+        append_buffered(c1, buff)
+    end
+    return c1
+end
+
+function append_unbuffered(c::Channel, iter)
+    for v in iter
+        put!(c, v)
+    end
+    return c
+end
+

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation
 min    3.267 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 44 samples with 1 evaluation

+"""
+    take!(c::Channel, n::Integer[, buffer::AbstractVector])
+
+Take at most `n` items from the [`Channel`](@ref) `c` and return them in a `Vector`.
+If `buffer` is provided, it will be used to store the result. If the channel closes before
+`n` items are taken, the result will contain only the items that were available. For
+buffered channels, this operation requires fewer `lock` operations than individual `take!(c)`s.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c, 3, zeros(Int, 3))
+3-element Vector{Int64}:
+ 1
+ 2
+ 3
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function take!(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer) where {T}
+    return _take(c, n, Vector{T}(undef, n))
+end
+function take!(c::Channel, n::Integer, buffer::AbstractVector)
+    # buffer is user defined, so make sure it has the correct size
+    if length(buffer) != n
+        resize!(buffer, n)
+    end","path":"base/channels.jl","commit_id":"8203cd857314b0605a7b1b01460f5c1dbacdd048","original_commit_id":"8203cd857314b0605a7b1b01460f5c1dbacdd048","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"These lines prevent you from doing `take(channel, typemax(Int), buffer)` as an idiom to fill all elements of the channel into the buffer. `append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation
 min    3.267 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 median 3.525 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

+"""
+    take!(c::Channel, n::Integer[, buffer::AbstractVector])
+
+Take at most `n` items from the [`Channel`](@ref) `c` and return them in a `Vector`.
+If `buffer` is provided, it will be used to store the result. If the channel closes before
+`n` items are taken, the result will contain only the items that were available. For
+buffered channels, this operation requires fewer `lock` operations than individual `take!(c)`s.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c, 3, zeros(Int, 3))
+[1,2,3]
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function take!(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer) where {T}
+    return _take(c, n, Vector{T}(undef, n))
+end
+function take!(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer, buffer::AbstractVector{T2}) where `append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation
 min    3.267 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 median 3.525 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

+"""
+    append!(c::Channel, iter)
+
+Append all items in `iter` to the channel `c`. If the channel is buffered, this operation requires
+fewer `lock` operations than individual `put!`s. Blocks if the channel is full.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c)
+1
+
+julia> take!(c)
+2
+
+julia> take!(c)
+3
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function append!(c::Channel, iter::T) where {T}
+    if hasmethod(length, Tuple{T})
+        len = length(iter)
+        # shortcircuit for small short objects
+        if len == 0
+            return c
+        elseif len == 1
+            put!(c, @inbounds vec[begin])
+            return c
+        end
+    end
+
+    return isbuffered(c) ? append_buffered(c, iter) : append_unbuffered(c, iter)
+end
+function append!(c1::Channel, c2::Channel{T}) where {T}
+    buff_len = max(c1.sz_max, c2.sz_max)
+    if buff_len == 0
+        return append_unbuffered(c1, c2)
+    end
+
+    buff = Vector{T}(undef, buff_len)
+    while isopen(c2) || isready(c2)
+        take!(c2, buff_len, buff)
+        append_buffered(c1, buff)
+    end
+    return c1
+end
+
+function append_unbuffered(c::Channel, iter)
+    for v in iter
+        put!(c, v)
+    end
+    return c
+end
+
+function append_buffered(c::Channel{T}, iter::I) where {T,I}","path":"base/channels.jl","commit_id":"f71759483377720ac64573e731a6547a84dc73cc","original_commit_id":"8203cd857314b0605a7b1b01460f5c1dbacdd048","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"It's only supposed to work with iterable types, right? `append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation
 min    3.267 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 median 3.525 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 mean   4.724 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 max    15.609 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 44 samples with 1 evaluation

 median 1.889 ms (128 allocs: 780.312 KiB)
 mean   2.168 ms (128 allocs: 780.312 KiB)
 max    7.967 ms (128 allocs: 780.312 KiB)

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation
 min    3.267 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 median 3.525 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 mean   4.724 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 max    15.609 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 44 samples with 1 evaluation

ttps://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/28694980?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbaraldi","html_url":"https://github.com/gbaraldi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbaraldi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbaraldi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbaraldi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbaraldi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbaraldi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbaraldi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbaraldi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbaraldi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbaraldi/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false}],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-11-11T14:40:01Z","updated_at":"2024-11-11T18:45:38Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I accidentally stumbled on the following:
```jl
julia> v = Union{Vector{Int}, Nothing}[Vector{Int}[] for _ in 1:3]
```
This returns a `Vector{Union{Vector{Int}, Nothing}}` but, really, I think it should error. The issue is that the elements in `[Vector{Int}[] for _ in 1:3]` are _not_ `Vector{Int}` but `Vector{Vector{Int}}`, so we have scenario of the kind `Union{A, B}[c::C for c in ...]` with `C` not a subtype of `A` or `B`.

The reason this works is that each of the `Vector{Int}[]` elements are passed on to the conversion machinery. In particular, as pointed out by @thofma on Slack, this is probably passed to one of these methods:

```jl
julia> convert(Vector{Int}, Vector{Int}[]) # Int64[]
julia> Vector{Int}(Vector{Int}[]) # Int64[]
```

It's hard to see where this would be desirable though. Additionally, the conversion only works if the to-be-converted-array is empty. That is, the following fails:
```jl
julia> convert(Vector{Int}, Vector{Int}[[1,2,3],[4,5]])
julia> Vector{Int}(Vector{Int}[[1,2,3],[4,5]])
```
It seems strange and error-prone to have conversion methods that only work for empty input.

merge sort, as an obvious choice for a stable sort of tuples.

A recursive data structure of singleton type, representing Peano natural numbers, is used to help with splitting a tuple into two halves in the merge sort. An alternative design would use a reference tuple, but this would require relying on `tail` more, which seems more harsh on the compiler. With the recursive datastructure the predecessor operation and the successor operation are both trivial.

Allows inference to preserve inferred element type even when tuple length is not known.

Follow-up PRs may add further improvements, such as the ability to select an unstable sorting algorithm.

The added file, typedomainnumbers.jl is not specific to sorting, thus making it a separate file. Xref #55571.

Fixes #54489","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56425/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56425/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2469957483","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56425#issuecomment-2469957483","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56425","id":2469957483,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6TOI9r","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-12T09:02:00Z","updated_at":"2024-11-12T09:02:00Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Do we even want to be able to sort tuples? {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56568","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56568/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56568/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56568/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56568","id":2660553081,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6elNF5","number":56568,"title":"how to define a more general `unique` function conveniently?","user":{"login":"WalterMadelim","id":143272683,"node_id":"U_kgDOCIoq6w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/143272683?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim","html_url":"https://github.com/WalterMadelim","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WalterMadelim/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-11-15T02:54:17Z","updated_at":"2024-11-15T08:12:20Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"`unique` function is based on `isequal` function.
If `isequal` function can be user-defined, we can also get a resultant new `unique` function. This should be convenient. 
But how to achieve this? Currently I don't know the way. See the following code.

```julia
julia> isequal(2, 3)
false

julia> unique([2, 3, 5, 2])   # 3 is different from 2, hence it remains
3-element Vector{Int64}:
 2
 3
 5

julia> Base.isequal(m::Int, n::Int) = ( abs(m - n) <= 1 ? true : false )  # user-define a new method

julia> isequal(2, 3)
true

julia> unique([2, 3, 5, 2])  # 3 is now deemed equates to 2, hence it should be removed ???
3-element Vector{Int64}:
 2
 3
 5
```

It would be wonderful if we have such a keyword choice:
```julia
julia> a, b = rand(3, 2), rand(3, 2);

julia> norm1(x) = LinearAlgebra.norm(x, 1);

julia> norm1(a .- b)
0.43189600452632304

julia> isapprox(a, b; atol = 0.42, norm = norm1)
false

julia> isapprox(a, b; atol = 0.44, norm = norm1)
true

julia> my_criterion = (a, b) -> isapprox(a, b; atol = 0.42, norm = norm1)
#7 (generic function with 1 method)

julia> my_criterion(a, b)
false

julia> unique([a, b, 2*a, 2*b]; criterion = my_criterion)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching unique(::Vector{Matrix{Float64}}; criterion::var"#7#8")
```

1. The definitions of `isequal` and `hash` must match in that `isequal(a, b)` implies `hash(a) == hash(b)`. Your definition of `isequal` violates this requirement
2. I've turned the open ended issue #54454 into an actual PR.
Tangentially related to #10092 ?

This PR introduces the `nth(itr, n)` function to iterators to give a `getindex` type of behaviour.
I've tried my best to optimize as much as possible by specializing on different types of iterators.
In the spirit of iterators any OOB access returns `nothing`.

here is the comparison of running the testsuite (~22 different iterators)  using generic `nth` and specialized `nth`:
```julia
@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
         _fallback_nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
117.750 μs (366 allocations: 17.88 KiB)

@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
    nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
24.250 μs (341 allocations: 16.70 KiB)
```","created_at":"2024-11-16T12:17:36Z","updated_at":"2024-11-16T13:28:04Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"merge_commit_sha":"724dd8e0e18dfc47d91e558038f1b896e248354b","assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[],"milestone":null,"draft":false,"commits_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/56580/commits","review_comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/56580/comments","review_comment_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments{/number}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56580/comments","statuses_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/statuses/11ee27a31310d88d4606d8325eafd36fb2559712","head":{"label":"ghyatzo:nth-api","ref":"nth-api","sha":"11ee27a31310d88d4606d8325eafd36fb2559712","user":{"login":"ghyatzo","id":8601724,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2MDE3MjQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8601724?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo","html_url":"https://github.com/ghyatzo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"repo":{"id":888070495,"node_id":"R_kgDONO7hXw","name":"julia","full_name":"ghyatzo/julia","private":false,"owner":{"login":"ghyatzo","id":8601724,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2MDE3MjQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubuserco  # be the same as the keys, so this is a valid optimization (see #51631)
 pairs(s::AbstractString) = IterableStatePairs(s)
 
+"""
+    nth(itr, n::Integer)
+
+Get the `n`th element of an iterable collection. Return `nothing` if not existing.
+
+See also: [`first`](@ref), [`last`](@ref)
+
+# Examples
+```jldoctest
+julia> nth(2:2:10, 4)
+8
+
+julia> nth(reshape(1:30, (5,6)), 6)
+6
+```
+"""
+nth(itr, n::Integer) = _nth(IteratorSize(itr), itr, n)
+nth(itr::AbstractArray, n::Integer) = n > length(itr) ? nothing : itr[n]","path":"base/iterators.jl","commit_id":"11ee27a31310d88d4606d8325eafd36fb2559712","original_commit_id":"11ee27a31310d88d4606d8325eafd36fb2559712","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This assumes one-based indexing. I've turned the open ended issue #54454 into an actual PR.
Tangentially related to #10092 ?

This PR introduces the `nth(itr, n)` function to iterators to give a `getindex` type of behaviour.
I've tried my best to optimize as much as possible by specializing on different types of iterators.
In the spirit of iterators any OOB access returns `nothing`.

here is the comparison of running the testsuite (~22 different iterators)  using generic `nth` and specialized `nth`:
```julia
@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
         _fallback_nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
117.750 μs (366 allocations: 17.88 KiB)

@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
    nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
24.250 μs (341 allocations: 16.70 KiB)

 pairs(s::AbstractString) = IterableStatePairs(s)
 
+"""
+    nth(itr, n::Integer)
+
+Get the `n`th element of an iterable collection. Return `nothing` if not existing.
+
+See also: [`first`](@ref), [`last`](@ref)
+
+# Examples
+```jldoctest
+julia> nth(2:2:10, 4)
+8
+
+julia> nth(reshape(1:30, (5,6)), 6)
+6
+```
+"""
+nth(itr, n::Integer) = _nth(IteratorSize(itr), itr, n)
+nth(itr::AbstractArray, n::Integer) = n > length(itr) ? nothing : itr[n]
+
+_nth(::SizeUnknown, itr, n) = _fallback_nth(itr, n)
+_nth(::Union{HasShape,HasLength}, itr, n) = _withlength_nth(itr, n, length(itr))
+_nth(::IsInfinite, itr, n) = _inbounds_nth(itr, n)
+
+_inbounds_nth(itr, n) = iterate(drop(itr, n - 1))[1]
+_inbounds_nth(itr::AbstractArray, n) = I've turned the open ended issue #54454 into an actual PR.
Tangentially related to #10092 ?

This PR introduces the `nth(itr, n)` function to iterators to give a `getindex` type of behaviour.
I've tried my best to optimize as much as possible by specializing on different types of iterators.
In the spirit of iterators any OOB access returns `nothing`.

here is the comparison of running the testsuite (~22 different iterators)  using generic `nth` and specialized `nth`:
```julia
@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
         _fallback_nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
117.750 μs (366 allocations: 17.88 KiB)

@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
    nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
24.250 μs (341 allocations: 16.70 KiB)

I've turned the open ended issue #54454 into an actual PR.
Tangentially related to #10092 ?

This PR introduces the `nth(itr, n)` function to iterators to give a `getindex` type of behaviour.
I've tried my best to optimize as much as possible by specializing on different types of iterators.
In the spirit of iterators any OOB access returns `nothing`.

here is the comparison of running the testsuite (~22 different iterators)  using generic `nth` and specialized `nth`:
```julia
@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
         _fallback_nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
117.750 μs (366 allocations: 17.88 KiB)

@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
    nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
24.250 μs (341 allocations: 16.70 KiB)

I've turned the open ended issue #54454 into an actual PR.
Tangentially related to #10092 ?

This PR introduces the `nth(itr, n)` function to iterators to give a `getindex` type of behaviour.
I've tried my best to optimize as much as possible by specializing on different types of iterators.
In the spirit of iterators any OOB access returns `nothing`.

here is the comparison of running the testsuite (~22 different iterators)  using generic `nth` and specialized `nth`:
```julia
@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
         _fallback_nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
117.750 μs (366 allocations: 17.88 KiB)

@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
    nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
24.250 μs (341 allocations: 16.70 KiB)

I've turned the open ended issue #54454 into an actual PR.
Tangentially related to #10092 ?

This PR introduces the `nth(itr, n)` function to iterators to give a `getindex` type of behaviour.
I've tried my best to optimize as much as possible by specializing on different types of iterators.
In the spirit of iterators any OOB access returns `nothing`.

here is the comparison of running the testsuite (~22 different iterators)  using generic `nth` and specialized `nth`:
```julia
@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
         _fallback_nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
117.750 μs (366 allocations: 17.88 KiB)

@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
    nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
24.250 μs (341 allocations: 16.70 KiB)

I've turned the open ended issue #54454 into an actual PR.
Tangentially related to #10092 ?

This PR introduces the `nth(itr, n)` function to iterators to give a `getindex` type of behaviour.
I've tried my best to optimize as much as possible by specializing on different types of iterators.
In the spirit of iterators any OOB access returns `nothing`.

here is the comparison of running the testsuite (~22 different iterators)  using generic `nth` and specialized `nth`:
```julia
@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
         _fallback_nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
117.750 μs (366 allocations: 17.88 KiB)

@btime begin                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  for (itr, n, _) in $testset                                                                                                                                                                                           
    nth(itr, n)                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  end                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
end                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
24.250 μs (341 allocations: 16.70 KiB)
