If you remove e.g. the element at index 2 from a vector, then what was previously at element 3 will move down to be in position 2. This is the same as an ordinary Vector.
A sparse vector is not like a Dict - it's rather an alternative implementation of something like Vector. If the indices were preserved, then no elements would have been removed, which would have been wrong according to the docstring.
In other words, the elements not printed when you display the sparse vector are not missing elements. They exist, but are zero. This is different from a Dict.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2037455142/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1557150993","pull_request_review_id":1988372696,"id":1557150993,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5c0D0R","diff_hunk":"@@ -62,7 +62,20 @@ to an output stream, or `ncodeunits(string(c))` but computed efficiently.
     This method requires at least Julia 1.1. In Julia 1.0 consider
     using `ncodeunits(string(c))`.
 """
-ncodeunits(c::Char) = write(devnull, c) # this is surprisingly efficient
+function ncodeunits(c::Char)
+    # All Char are 4 byte wide, and since unicode encoding
+    # doesn't have null bytes (except for \0), we can just
+    # count non-zero bytes
+    char_data = reinterpret(UInt32, c)
+    mask = 0xff % UInt32
+    nbytes = !iszero(char_data & mask)
+    Base.Cartesian.@nexprs 3 i -> begin
+        m <<= 0x8
+        nbytes += !iszero(char_data & mask)
+    end
+    # We have to account for `\0`, which is encoded as all zeros
+    nbytes + iszero(uc)","path":"base/char.jl","commit_id":"3e9520aa0efe74b0866d1dcbeffd580e8163bd24","original_commit_id":"3e9520aa0efe74b0866d1dcbeffd580e8163bd24","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"```suggestion

`ncodeunits(::Char)`","user":{"login":"Seelengrab","id":11753998,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzUzOTk4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11753998?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab","html_url":"https://github.com/Seelengrab","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This improves performance of `ncodeunits(::Char)` by simply counting the number of non-zero bytes (except for `\0`, which is encoded as all zero bytes). For a performance comparison, see [this gist](
 https://gist.github.com/Seelengrab/ebb02d4b8d754700c2869de8daf88cad); there's an up to 10x improvement here for collections of `Char`, with a minor improvement for  single `Char` (with much smaller spread). The version in this PR is called `nbytesencoded` in the benchmarks.

Correctness has been verified with Supposition.jl, using the existing implementation as an oracle:

```julia
julia> using Supposition

julia> const chars = Data.Characters()

julia> @check max_examples=1_000_000 function bytesenc(c=chars)
           ncodeunits(c) == nbytesencoded(c)
       end;
Test Summary: | Pass  Total  Time
bytesenc      |    1      1  1.2s

julia> ncodeunits('\0') == nbytesencoded('\0')
true
```

Let's see if CI agrees!

Notably, neither the existing nor the new implementation check whether the given `Char` is valid or not, since the only thing that matters is how many bytes are written      This method requires at least Julia 1.1. In Julia 1.0 consider
     using `ncodeunits(string(c))`.
 """
-ncodeunits(c::Char) = write(devnull, c) # this is surprisingly efficient
+function ncodeunits(c::Char)
+    # All Char are 4 byte wide, and since unicode encoding
+    # doesn't have null bytes (except for \0), we can just
+    # count non-zero bytes
+    char_data = reinterpret(UInt32, c)
+    mask = 0xff % UInt32
+    nbytes = !iszero(char_data & mask)
+    Base.Cartesian.@nexprs 3 i -> begin
+        m <<= 0x8","path":"base/char.jl","commit_id":"3e9520aa0efe74b0866d1dcbeffd580e8163bd24","original_commit_id":"3e9520aa0efe74b0866d1dcbeffd580e8163bd24","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"```suggestion

`ncodeunits(::Char)`","user":{"login":"Seelengrab","id":11753998,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzUzOTk4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11753998?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab","html_url":"https://github.com/Seelengrab","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This improves performance of `ncodeunits(::Char)` by simply counting the number of non-zero bytes (except for `\0`, which is encoded as all zero bytes). For a performance comparison, see [this gist](
 https://gist.github.com/Seelengrab/ebb02d4b8d754700c2869de8daf88cad); there's an up to 10x improvement here for collections of `Char`, with a minor improvement for  single `Char` (with much smaller spread). The version in this PR is called `nbytesencoded` in the benchmarks.

Correctness has been verified with Supposition.jl, using the existing implementation as an oracle:

```julia
julia> using Supposition

julia> const chars = Data.Characters()

julia> @check max_examples=1_000_000 function bytesenc(c=chars)
           ncodeunits(c) == nbytesencoded(c)
       end;
Test Summary: | Pass  Total  Time
bytesenc      |    1      1  1.2s

julia> ncodeunits('\0') == nbytesencoded('\0')
true
```

Let's see if CI agrees!

Notably, neither the existing nor the new implementation check whether the given `Char` is valid or not, since the only thing that matters is how many bytes are written improves performance of `ncodeunits(::Char)` by simply counting the number of non-zero bytes (except for `\0`, which is encoded as all zero bytes). For a performance comparison, see [this gist](
 https://gist.github.com/Seelengrab/ebb02d4b8d754700c2869de8daf88cad); there's an up to 10x improvement here for collections of `Char`, with a minor improvement for  single `Char` (with much smaller spread). The version in this PR is called `nbytesencoded` in the benchmarks.

Correctness has been verified with Supposition.jl, using the existing implementation as an oracle:

```julia
julia> using Supposition

julia> const chars = Data.Characters()

julia> @check max_examples=1_000_000 function bytesenc(c=chars)
           ncodeunits(c) == nbytesencoded(c)
       end;
Test Summary: | Pass  Total  Time
bytesenc      |    1      1  1.2s

julia> ncodeunits('\0') == nbytesencoded('\0')
true
```

Let's see if CI agrees!

Notably, neither the existing nor the new implementation check whether the given `Char` is valid or not, since the only thing that matters is how many bytes are written improves performance of `ncodeunits(::Char)` by simply counting the number of non-zero bytes (except for `\0`, which is encoded as all zero bytes). For a performance comparison, see [this gist](
 https://gist.github.com/Seelengrab/ebb02d4b8d754700c2869de8daf88cad); there's an up to 10x improvement here for collections of `Char`, with a minor improvement for  single `Char` (with much smaller spread). The version in this PR is called `nbytesencoded` in the benchmarks.

Correctness has been verified with Supposition.jl, using the existing implementation as an oracle:

```julia
julia> using Supposition

julia> const chars = Data.Characters()

julia> @check max_examples=1_000_000 function bytesenc(c=chars)
           ncodeunits(c) == nbytesencoded(c)
       end;
Test Summary: | Pass  Total  Time
bytesenc      |    1      1  1.2s

julia> ncodeunits('\0') == nbytesencoded('\0')
true
```

Let's see if CI agrees!

Notably, neither the existing nor the new implementation check whether the given `Char` is valid or not, since the only thing that matters is how many bytes are written `ncodeunits(::Char)`","user":{"login":"Seelengrab","id":11753998,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzUzOTk4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11753998?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab","html_url":"https://github.com/Seelengrab","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This improves performance of `ncodeunits(::Char)` by simply counting the number of non-zero bytes (except for `\0`, which is encoded as all zero bytes). For a performance comparison, see [this gist](
 https://gist.github.com/Seelengrab/ebb02d4b8d754700c2869de8daf88cad); there's an up to 10x improvement here for collections of `Char`, with a minor improvement for  single `Char` (with much smaller spread). The version in this PR is called `nbytesencoded` in the benchmarks.

Correctness has been verified with Supposition.jl, using the existing implementation as an oracle:

```julia
julia> using Supposition

julia> const chars = Data.Characters()

julia> @check max_examples=1_000_000 function bytesenc(c=chars)
           ncodeunits(c) == nbytesencoded(c)
       end;
Test Summary: | Pass  Total  Time
bytesenc      |    1      1  1.2s

julia> ncodeunits('\0') == nbytesencoded('\0')
true
```

Let's see if CI agrees!

Notably, neither the existing nor the new implementation check whether the given `Char` is valid or not, since the only thing that matters is how many bytes are written faster"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-04-09T07:23:49Z","updated_at":"2024-04-09T08:30:46Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54001","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54001","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54001.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54001.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This improves performance of `ncodeunits(::Char)` by simply counting the number of non-zero bytes (except for `\0`, which is encoded as all zero bytes). For a performance comparison, see [this gist](
 https://gist.github.com/Seelengrab/ebb02d4b8d754700c2869de8daf88cad); there's an up to 10x improvement here for collections of `Char`, with a minor improvement for  single `Char` (with much smaller spread). The version in this PR is called `nbytesencoded` in the benchmarks.

Correctness has been verified with Supposition.jl, using the existing implementation as an oracle:

```julia
julia> using Supposition

julia> const chars = Data.Characters()

julia> @check max_examples=1_000_000 function bytesenc(c=chars)
           ncodeunits(c) == nbytesencoded(c)
       end;
Test Summary: | Pass  Total  Time
bytesenc      |    1      1  1.2s

julia> ncodeunits('\0') == nbytesencoded('\0')
true
```

Let's see if CI agrees!

Notably, neither the existing nor the new implementation check whether the given `Char` is valid or not, since the only thing that matters is how many bytes are written out.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54001/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54001/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2044440595","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54001#issuecomment-2044440595","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54001","id":2044440595,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5527AT","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-09T08:30:45Z","updated_at":"2024-04-09T08:30:45Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Also this implementation is incorrect - The idea was that randomly generated chars have an unrealistic distribution of codepoint lengths, and therefore unfairly punishes branchful code since the branch predictor will be much better on real strings with a more predictable number of codeunits per char.

improves performance of `ncodeunits(::Char)` by simply counting the number of non-zero bytes (except for `\0`, which is encoded as all zero bytes). For a performance comparison, see [this gist](
 https://gist.github.com/Seelengrab/ebb02d4b8d754700c2869de8daf88cad); there's an up to 10x improvement here for collections of `Char`, with a minor improvement for  single `Char` (with much smaller spread). The version in this PR is called `nbytesencoded` in the benchmarks.

Correctness has been verified with Supposition.jl, using the existing implementation as an oracle:

```julia
julia> using Supposition

julia> const chars = Data.Characters()

julia> @check max_examples=1_000_000 function bytesenc(c=chars)
           ncodeunits(c) == nbytesencoded(c)
       end;
Test Summary: | Pass  Total  Time
bytesenc      |    1      1  1.2s

julia> ncodeunits('\0') == nbytesencoded('\0')
true
```

Let's see if CI agrees!

Notably, neither the existing nor the new implementation check whether the given `Char` is valid or not, since the only thing that matters is how many bytes are written out.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54001/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54001/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2044740615","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54001#issuecomment-2044740615","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54001","id":2044740615,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM554EQH","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-09T11:08:00Z","updated_at":"2024-04-09T11:08:00Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Yep, {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54118","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54118/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54118/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54118/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54118","id":2249193737,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6GD_kJ","number":54118,"title":"Add functions like findonly, uniqueonly, ...","user":{"login":"aplavin","id":687995,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4Nzk5NQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/687995?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin","html_url":"https://github.com/aplavin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-04-17T20:14:26Z","updated_at":"2024-04-18T06:29:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'd like to propose adding a bunch of simple functions to Julia:
- `findonly`: complements `findfirst` and `findlast`, throws if there are multiple matches
- `filterfirst`, `filteronly`, maybe `filterlast`: same as `first/last/only(filter(pred, collection))` but faster
- `uniqueonly`: like `only(unique(collection))`, but faster

These are straightforward to implement, and the motivation to include them is similar to that of `only()`: mostly for safety and convenience.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54118/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54118/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2063095696","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54118#issuecomment-2063095696","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54118","id":2063095696,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM56-FeQ","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-18T06:29:47Z","updated_at":"2024-04-18T06:29:47Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"In my opinion, `findonly`, `filterfirst`, `filteronly` and `filterlast` are too trivial to be a good idea. These can straightforwardly be written in terms of lazy operations which are much more generically useful that what's proposed here:
* `findonly -> Iterators.findall + only`
* `filterfirst -> Iterators.filter + first`
* `filteronly -> Iterators.filter + only`
* `filterlast -> Iterators.reverse + Iterators.filter + first`

The only issues with these I see are:
1. We don't currently have `Iterators.findall` (#43737)
2. It's slightly annoying that the most useful versions of `filter`, `reverse`, etc. are in the `Iterators` namespace.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2063095696/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

     IdSet()
 
 IdSet{T}() constructs a set (see [`Set`](@ref)) using
-`===` as equality with values of type `V`.
+`===` as equality with values of type `T`.
 
-In the example below, the values are all `isequal` so they get overwritten.
-The `IdSet` compares by `===` so preserves the 3 different keys.
-
-Examples
-≡≡≡≡≡≡≡≡
+In the example below, the values are all `isequal` so they get overwritten in the ordinary `Set`.
+The `IdSet` compares by `===` and so preserves the 3 different values.

Functions like `isfile` should operate on paths, that's it. They should not operate on dicts, sets, lists, `Nothing`s, or just any old random value. This just makes semantic sense. Since we (unfortunately) don't have a path type in Julia, it'll have to be strings instead.

I think if people want to invent new data types like to represent paths, they'll have to add new methods themselves. I don't think that's unreasonable to expect.
It's possible I misunderstood the motivation for allowing `::Any` in these methods, but I really don't see the point. Integers aren't paths. Neither are linked lists, or matrices, etc.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2068806110/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The failures you link to, which seem less significant, are just the first to fail in their test suite.

* For PyCall, `pybytes` is a user-facing function that no longer work on codeunits. Hence, this will break scripts that call `pybytes` to pass it into Python. This is shown as an example on the README.md of PyCall, so I'd call this breakage.
* For `StringView`s, a large part of the functionality of the whole package is defined as methods with `::DenseStringView`, which relies on codeunits being a subtype of dense vector. I've now made the following changes:
* Print the type of type (`struct`, `primitive type` etc.) at the beginning before the name
* Don't print `const` unless in a mutable struct.

Here are some examples:
```
julia> dump(Float32)
primitive type Float32 <: AbstractFloat

julia> dump(Signed)
abstract type Signed <: Integer

julia> dump(Pair{Int, String})
struct Pair{Int64, String} <: Any
  first::Int64
  second::String

julia> dump(BitSet)
mutable struct BitSet <: AbstractSet{Int64}
  const bits::Vector{UInt64}
  offset::Int64

julia> dump(Set)
UnionAll
  var: TypeVar
    name: Symbol T
    lb: Union{}
    ub: abstract type Any
  body: struct Set{T} <: AbstractSet{T}
    dict::Dict{T, Nothing}
```","created_at":"2024-03-01T07:26:08Z","updated_at":"2024-03-01T07:26:08Z","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53492#discussion_r1508596137","pull_request_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53492","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","_links":{"self":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1508596137"},"html":{"href":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53492#discussion_r1508596137"},"pull_request":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53492"}},"reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1508596137/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"start_line":null,"original_start_line":null,"start_side":null,"line":1266,"original_line":1266,"side":"LEFT","in_reply_to_id":1505508223,"original_position":4,"position":8,"subject_type":"line"},"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53492","id":1745569681,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5oC0eR","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53492","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53492.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53492.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53492","number":53492,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Make provides more information to the user when dumping types, and also makes the output of dump slightly more similar to the type definition syntax.

Before:
```
julia> dump(BitSet)
BitSet <: AbstractSet{Int64}
  bits::Vector{UInt64}
  offset::Int64
```

After:
```
julia> dump(BitSet)
BitSet <: AbstractSet{Int64}
  const bits::Vector{UInt64}
  offset::Int64

"subproject" feature","user":{"login":"KristofferC","id":1282691,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODI2OTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1282691?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC","html_url":"https://github.com/KristofferC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-03-12T15:27:59Z","updated_at":"2024-03-13T11:37:39Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pulls/3841","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/3841","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/3841.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/3841.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR requires https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53653.

This allows one to define a subproject which is an incremental addition to the dependency graph of the "base project". When the sub project is active it can load any dependency from the base project and the sub project itself. When Pkg resolves, it resolves among the base project and all sub projects and the full dependency graph is stored in a single manifest next to the base project's manifest.

Subprojects are defined by specifying a list of folders in the project file as:

```
subprojects = ["test", "benchmarks"]
```

Right now, the base project has to be one level above any sub project.

TODO: 

- [x] More thorough tests especially regarding merging of compats and sources among projects.
- [x] Docs

As a follow-up to this I want to make it so that the test subproject is special and that running `Pkg.test` if this is active should be equivalent to:

```
Pkg.activate("test")
Pkg.resolve()
include("test/runtests.jl")
```

Other follow up is to perhaps enhance `Pkg.status()` to show more clearly what packages comes from the base project and what comes from the sub project.

* In a project where the main project has Automa added with compat "1", and the sub-project has Automa added with compat "=1.0.2" (not the newest version), when showing `(mypkg) pkg> status`, it falsely displays that Automa can be upgraded:
```
⌃ [67c07d97] Automa v1.0.2
```
This also occurs the other way around: If the parent project has a restrictive dependency but the subproject does not, the subproject's version is held back, but the green `^` still appears in the subproject's `status`, suggesting the package can be updated.

* When the subproject depends on `BenchmarkTools` but the parent does not, `BenchmarkTools` shows up in `pkg> status`. However, it's not part of the project, nor can it be loaded. behavior"},{"id":9159719,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5MTU5NzE5","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/parallel","name":"parallel","color":"f7e102","default":false,"description":"Parallel computation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-01-19T09:54:05Z","updated_at":"2024-02-01T07:12:43Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE:
```julia
julia> collect(walkdir("dsaasds"))
Tuple{String, Vector{String}, Vector{String}}[]
```

I believe this is a bug, per the docstring of `walkdir`, which promises:
>  If walkdir or stat encounters a IOError it will rethrow the error by default.

In the code above, it should raise:
```julia
julia> readdir("dsaasds")
ERROR: IOError: readdir("dsaasds"): no such file or directory (ENOENT)
```

The bug happens due to the following code in `walkdir`. The context is that `walkdir` returns a `Channel`, in the code called `chnl`:
```julia
try
    f(p) # a readdir call
catch err
    isa(err, IOError) || rethrow()
    try
        onerror(err) # this is throw(err)
    catch err2
        close(chnl, err2)
    end
    return
end
```
So this happens:
* `f(p)` raises an `IOError`
* `onerror(err)` occurs in the inner try block, throwing the error again
* This is caught by the second `catch` block, closing the channel with this error. This is wrong, because the error is never thrown

Here is another variation on the same bug. Here I have
```
.
└── bar
   └── <Permission denied (os error 13)>
```
(i.e. I have read access to `.` but not to `bar`). Then:
```julia
julia> w = walkdir(".");

julia> iterate(w)
((".", ["bar"], String[]), nothing)

julia> iterate(w) # returns nothing, but should throw

julia> collect(walkdir(".")) # somehow, throws here
ERROR: IOError: readdir("./bar"): permission denied (EACCES
```

For example, a method which previously took
```julia
Union{
    Array{UInt8},
    CodeUnits{UInt8, String},
    FastContiguousSubArray{UInt8,N,<:Array{UInt8}} where N,
}
```
Now also takes `Memory{UInt8}`, and the `Memory`-backed `FastContiguousSubArray`.

## Notes for reviewers
1. I don't think this is a particularly nice design - these large unions are a sign we miss an abstraction. In particular, whether something is a contiguous, memory backed Array (read or read/write). I think we should have a trait for this, which requires an implementation of e.g. `pointer` and `sizeof`. However, that's a larger PR for another time.

3. The functions `readbytes_some!` and `readbytes_all!` may resize its arguments. This will not work for `Memory`. It already didn't work for `SubArray`. Maybe we should have a different method for the arrays that can't be resized, which doesn't attempt commit changes the signature of a bunch of methods that operate on byte buffers, and which previously didn't take `Memory`. For example, a method which previously took
```julia
Union{
    Array{UInt8},
    CodeUnits{UInt8, String},
    FastContiguousSubArray{UInt8,N,<:Array{UInt8}} where N,
}
```
Now also takes `Memory{UInt8}`, and the `Memory`-backed `FastContiguousSubArray`.

## Notes for reviewers
1. I don't think this is a particularly nice design - these large unions are a sign we miss an abstraction. In particular, whether something is a contiguous, memory backed Array (read or read/write). I think we should have a trait for this, which requires an implementation of e.g. `pointer` and `sizeof`. However, that's a larger PR for another time.

3. The functions `readbytes_some!` and `readbytes_all!` may resize its arguments. This will not work for `Memory`. It already didn't work for `SubArray`. Maybe we should have a different method for the arrays that can't be resized, which doesn't attempt to.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53178/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53178/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1926407467","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53178#issuecomment-1926407467","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53178","id":1926407467,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5y0qUr","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-02-05T07:56:07Z","updated_at":"2024-02-05T07:56:07Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I've not switched to using `DenseArray` where applicable - commit changes the signature of a bunch of methods that operate on byte buffers, and which previously didn't take `Memory`. For example, a method which previously took
```julia
Union{
    Array{UInt8},
    CodeUnits{UInt8, String},
    FastContiguousSubArray{UInt8,N,<:Array{UInt8}} where N,
}
```
Now also takes `Memory{UInt8}`, and the `Memory`-backed `FastContiguousSubArray`.

## Notes for reviewers
1. I don't think this is a particularly nice design - these large unions are a sign we miss an abstraction. In particular, whether something is a contiguous, memory backed Array (read or read/write). I think we should have a trait for this, which requires an implementation of e.g. `pointer` and `sizeof`. However, that's a larger PR for another time.

3. The functions `readbytes_some!` and `readbytes_all!` may resize its arguments. This will not work for `Memory`. It already didn't work for `SubArray`. Maybe we should have a different method for the arrays that can't be resized, which doesn't attempt to.","created_at":"2024-02-04T20:05:28Z","updated_at":"2024-02-06T07:08:12Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"merge_commit_sha":"07a8c2841a0030c82f2d4fbe92ee0503bc5dca93","assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[{"id":115858,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU4NTg=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/performance","name":"performance","color":"d7e102","default":false,"description":"Must go faster"},{"id":6123668114,"node_id":"LA_kwDOABkWpM8AAAABbP_Ckg","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/awaiting%20review","name":"awaiting review","color":"25EC7B","default":false,"description":"PR is complete and seems ready to merge. sets"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-01-24T19:28:45Z","updated_at":"2024-02-06T07:42:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I want a non-mutating version of `append!` that puts two sequences together.


```jl
julia> (append(x::T, y::T) where T<: AbstractVector) = append!(copy(x), y);

julia> (append(x::T, y::T) where T<:AbstractString)= join([x, y]);

julia> append([:a, :b], [:c, :d])
4-element Vector{Symbol}:
 :a
 :b
 :c
 :d

julia> append("ab", "cd")
"acdb"
```

commit changes the signature of a bunch of methods that operate on byte buffers, and which previously didn't take `Memory`. For example, a method which previously took
```julia
Union{
    Array{UInt8},
    CodeUnits{UInt8, String},
    FastContiguousSubArray{UInt8,N,<:Array{UInt8}} where N,
}
```
Now also takes `Memory{UInt8}`, and the `Memory`-backed `FastContiguousSubArray`.

## Notes for reviewers
1. I don't think this is a particularly nice design - these large unions are a sign we miss an abstraction. In particular, whether something is a contiguous, memory backed Array (read or read/write). I think we should have a trait for this, which requires an implementation of e.g. `pointer` and `sizeof`. However, that's a larger PR for another time.

3. The functions `readbytes_some!` and `readbytes_all!` may resize its arguments. This will not work for `Memory`. It already didn't work for `SubArray`. Maybe we should have a different method for the arrays that can't be resized, which doesn't attempt to.","created_at":"2024-02-04T20:05:28Z","updated_at":"2024-02-07T08:01:32Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"merge_commit_sha":"258670c2d552f7ece7906c4e1a9a40826d2bcd91","assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[{"id":115858,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU4NTg=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/performance","name":"performance","color":"d7e102","default":false,"description":"Must go faster"},{"id":6123668114,"node_id":"LA_kwDOABkWpM8AAAABbP_Ckg","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/awaiting%20review","name":"awaiting review","color":"25EC7B","default":false,"description":"PR is complete and seems ready to merge. commit changes the signature of a bunch of methods that operate on byte buffers, and which previously didn't take `Memory`. For example, a method which previously took
```julia
Union{
    Array{UInt8},
    CodeUnits{UInt8, String},
    FastContiguousSubArray{UInt8,N,<:Array{UInt8}} where N,
}
```
Now also takes `Memory{UInt8}`, and the `Memory`-backed `FastContiguousSubArray`.

## Notes for reviewers
1. I don't think this is a particularly nice design - these large unions are a sign we miss an abstraction. In particular, whether something is a contiguous, memory backed Array (read or read/write). I think we should have a trait for this, which requires an implementation of e.g. `pointer` and `sizeof`. However, that's a larger PR for another time.

3. The functions `readbytes_some!` and `readbytes_all!` may resize its arguments. This will not work for `Memory`. It already didn't work for `SubArray`. Maybe we should have a different method for the arrays that can't be resized, which doesn't attempt to.","created_at":"2024-02-04T20:05:28Z","updated_at":"2024-02-07T08:01:32Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"merge_commit_sha":"258670c2d552f7ece7906c4e1a9a40826d2bcd91","assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[{"id":115858,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU4NTg=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/performance","name":"performance","color":"d7e102","default":false,"description":"Must go faster"},{"id":6123668114,"node_id":"LA_kwDOABkWpM8AAAABbP_Ckg","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/awaiting%20review","name":"awaiting review","color":"25EC7B","default":false,"description":"PR is complete and seems ready to merge. CI failures unrelated."}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2024-02-04T20:05:28Z","updated_at":"2024-02-13T19:42:31Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53178","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53178","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53178.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53178.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This commit changes the signature of a bunch of methods that operate on byte buffers, and which previously didn't take `Memory`. For example, a method which previously took
```julia
Union{
    Array{UInt8},
    CodeUnits{UInt8, String},
    FastContiguousSubArray{UInt8,N,<:Array{UInt8}} where N,
}
```
Now also takes `Memory{UInt8}`, and the `Memory`-backed `FastContiguousSubArray`.

## Notes for reviewers
1. I don't think this is a particularly nice design - these large unions are a sign we miss an abstraction. In particular, whether something is a contiguous, memory backed Array (read or read/write). I think we should have a trait for this, which requires an implementation of e.g. `pointer` and `sizeof`. However, that's a larger PR for another time.

3. The functions `readbytes_some!` and `readbytes_all!` may resize its arguments. This will not work for `Memory`. It already didn't work for `SubArray`. Maybe we should have a different method for the arrays that can't be resized, which doesn't attempt to.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53178/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53178/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1942279100","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53178#issuecomment-1942279100","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53178","id":1942279100,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5zxNO8","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-02-13T19:42:30Z","updated_at":"2024-02-13T19:42:30Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"@stevengj the documentation of DenseArray states is must be contiguous in memory. expression `"x".3` gives the following error message in the REPL:
```
julia> "x".3
ERROR: cannot document the following expression:

0.3
```

This is strange because `0.3` is not the original expression and inconsistent compared to the error for `"x".y` which is more readable:
```
julia> "x".y
ERROR: type String has no field y
```

Could this be updated to be more consistent between the two errors?

documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-02-22T16:22:27Z","updated_at":"2024-02-24T11:16:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The return type in the example below should be `Pair{String,<:Real}`
```julia 
julia> typejoin(Pair{String, Int}, Pair{String,Float64})
Pair{String}
```

It might be a feature, or a limitation. Then, the docstring is to be modified.
```julia
typejoin(T, S, ...)

Return the closest common ancestor of types T and S, i.e. the narrowest type from which they both inherit. Recurses on additional varargs.
```

Other example that looks like an inconsistent behavior
```julia
abstract type AbstractX end
struct X1 <: AbstractX end
struct Y1 <: AbstractX end

[X1(), Y1()] # Vector{AbstractX} (correct)
["A"=>X1(), "A"=>Y1()] # Vector{Pair{String, AbstractX}} (correct)
"A" .=> [X1(), Y1()] # Vector{Pair{String}} (too wide)
```

behaviours","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":115619,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU2MTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/doc","name":"doc","color":"02d7e1","default":false,"description":"This change adds or pertains to documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-02-27T13:31:31Z","updated_at":"2024-02-27T13:31:31Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Examples:
```julia
julia> isconst(BitSet, :ajkhfd) # non-existent field
true

julia> mutable struct Foo
           const x::Int
       end

julia> isconst(Union{BitSet, Foo}, 1) # is actually true, but type is Union
false
```

Neither of these behaviours are documented.
I think it would be better if it threw for non-`DataType` types, (since its result is meaningless when applied to other kinds of types), or perhaps returned some sentinel value like `nothing`.
At the very least, if the current edge case behaviour is considered best, then it should be documented.

behavior"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-02-27T13:31:31Z","updated_at":"2024-02-27T16:32:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Examples:
```julia
julia> isconst(BitSet, :ajkhfd) # non-existent field
true

julia> mutable struct Foo
           const x::Int
       end

julia> isconst(Union{BitSet, Foo}, 1) # is actually true, but type is Union
false
```

Neither of these behaviours are documented.
I think it would be better if it threw for non-`DataType` types, (since its result is meaningless when applied to other kinds of types), or perhaps returned some sentinel value like `nothing`.
At the very least, if the current edge case behaviour is considered best, then it should be documented.

Ah, I see. The purpose of the function is to check for consistent behaviour (for the compiler, perhaps?).
From a user POV, It's not very clear from the documentation that the intended use of the function is to answer "does loading this field result in a consistent value", rather than "is this field declared as a const of this type"? The documentation seems to imply the latter. And if that's the latter, then a non-existent field is certainly not declared const because it's not declared at all. {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52704","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52704/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52704/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52704/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52704","id":2062808281,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM568_TZ","number":52704,"title":"Feature request: Get encoding of `AbstractString`","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-01-02T18:45:56Z","updated_at":"2024-01-02T18:45:56Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I do a lot with high-performance programming with strings. When you do that, it's often more efficient to work on the underlying bytes. Luckily, Julia enables that with `codeunits`.

However, there is no way of knowing generically, given some `AbstractString`, what encoding it uses - i.e, what the result of `codeunits` _means_. That makes it difficult (impossible?) to write code using `codeunits` for `AbstractString`. Most implementations of `AbstractString` uses UTF8, such as `String`, `SubString{String}`, `StringView` (of StringViews.jl), the various types in InlineStrings.jl, and more. But this is not generally true.

I propose to include a trait function `encoding(::Type{<:AbstractString})::Symbol`. In Base, the default implementations should be:
```julia
encoding(::Type{<:AbstractString}) = :unknown
encoding(::Type{String}) = :utf8
encoding(::Type{<:SubString{T}}) where T = encoding(T)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52704/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52704/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":408,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pulls/408","id":1670235842,"node_id":"PR_kwDOGankvM5jjcbC","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/408","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/408.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/408.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/408","number":408,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Add modular operators +% -% >>% etc.","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"For the last ten years, there have been idle discussion about allowing a way for users to opt-in for explicitly overflowing integer operations. Julia's arithmetic operations do overflow by default, but it may in the future be possible to run Julia in a mode where overflows are detected, for testing purposes. If this is to be done, then there needs to be a way to signal that the user explicitly wants overflow, which is important in some low-level operations, such as hashing.
For bitshifting, Julias definition of e.g. >> checks for overflow, which leads to suboptimal code. Analogous to the opt-in overflowing arithmetic ops like +%, users ought to be able to opt-in to the more efficient overflowing shifts, which in situations where bitshifts are a performance bottleneck.

This impements the JuliaSyntax side of Keno's https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50790 (cc @Keno).

### Note for reviewers:
1. The tests currently fail, due to the test that the operators in JuliaSyntax matches `Base.isoperator` (presumably this is the flisp parser)? This is not a bug, this just means the flisp parser needs an update, too
2. Keno has already updated the flisp parser to accept e.g. `+%` in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50790. However, in this PR I also add the assignment operators `+%=`. More importantly, I also add overflowing bitshifts to address https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50225 (also see its linked issues). These overflowing bitshift operators also needs support in the flisp parser

MWE:
```julia
julia> code_native(+, Tuple{Int, Int})
        .text
        .file   "+"
        .globl  "julia_+_490"                   # -- Begin function julia_+_490
        .p2align        4, 0x90
        .type   "julia_+_490",@function
"julia_+_490":                          # @"julia_+_490"
; ┌ @ int.jl:87 within `+`
# %bb.0:                                # %top
        push    rbp
        mov     rbp, rsp
        lea     rax, [rdi + rsi]
        pop     rbp
        ret
.Lfunc_end0:
        .size   "julia_+_490", .Lfunc_end0-"julia_+_490"
; └
                                        # -- End function
        .section        ".note.GNU-stack","",@progbits

julia> code_native(+, Tuple{Int, Int}; debuginfo=:none, dump_module=false)
        .text
        push    rbp
        mov     rbp, rsp
        mov     rax, qword ptr [r13 + 16]
        mov     rax, qword ptr [rax + 16]
        mov     rax, qword ptr [rax]
        lea     rax, [rdi + rsi]
        pop     rbp
        ret
        nop     word ptr cs:[rax + rax]

{"action":"opened","number":52828,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/52828","id":1670449080,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5jkQe4","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52828","number":52828,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Add wrapping shift function","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"In Julia, bitshifting a bitinteger x by more bits than is present in x causes x to be set to zero. Also, negative bitshifts are supported. This might semantically be more correct (and the former also matches LLVM's definition of bitshifts), but it does not correspond to either x86 or AArch64 behaviour. The result is that Julia's bitshifts are not optimised to a single instruction.
In constrast, in the C language, bitshifting by more than the bitwidth is UB, which allows the compiler to assume it never happens, and optimise the shift to a single instruction.

This commit adds the wrapping shift functions >>% >>>% and <<%, which overflows if the shift is too high or negative. The overflow behaviour is explicitly not stated, but the implemented behaviour matches the native behaviour of x86 and AArch64.

This commit requires JuliaSyntax support, which will be implemented in a parallel PR to JuliaSyntax.

## TODO
- [ ] Do we want this? I really want this in Julia, but maybe triage can make a decision here
- [ ] I need to test if these function compiles down to a single instruction on AArch64. If anyone has an ARM computer and can help me check it, that'd be grand
- [ ] JuliaSyntax.jl needs to be updated to support the parsing of `>>%` etc. See https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/408
- [ ] Maybe the flisp parser also needs to be updated? Let me know if so.

needed"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-01-09T11:12:30Z","updated_at":"2024-01-09T11:13:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/52828","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"In Julia, bitshifting a bitinteger x by more bits than is present in x causes x to be set to zero. Also, negative bitshifts are supported. This might semantically be more correct (and the former also matches LLVM's definition of bitshifts), but it does not correspond to either x86 or AArch64 behaviour. The result is that Julia's bitshifts are not optimised to a single instruction.
In constrast, in the C language, bitshifting by more than the bitwidth is UB, which allows the compiler to assume it never happens, and optimise the shift to a single instruction.

This commit adds the wrapping shift functions >>% >>>% and <<%, which overflows if the shift is too high or negative. The overflow behaviour is explicitly not stated, but the implemented behaviour matches the native behaviour of x86 and AArch64.

This commit requires JuliaSyntax support, which will be implemented in a parallel PR to JuliaSyntax.

## TODO
- [ ] Do we want this? I really want this in Julia, but maybe triage can make a decision here
- [ ] I need to test if these function compiles down to a single instruction on AArch64. If anyone has an ARM computer and can help me check it, that'd be grand
- [ ] JuliaSyntax.jl needs to be updated to support the parsing of `>>%` etc. See https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/408
- [ ] Maybe the flisp parser also needs to be updated? Let me know if so.
- [ ] Make sure this doesn't interfere with bootstrapping - move out of `int.jl` if necessary

julia> code_native(+, Tuple{Int, Int}; debuginfo=:none, dump_module=false)
        .text
        push    rbp
        mov     rbp, rsp
        mov     rax, qword ptr [r13 + 16]
        mov     rax, qword ptr [rax + 16]
        mov     rax, qword ptr [rax]
        lea     rax, [rdi + rsi]
        pop     rbp
        ret

> If raw is false, uninteresting instructions (like the safepoint function prologue) are
       elided.

However, this is not the case
```julia
julia> code_native(+, Tuple{Int, Int}, dump_module=false, raw=false)
	.text
; ┌ @ int.jl:87 within `+`
	push	rbp
	mov	rbp, rsp
	mov	rax, qword ptr [r13 + 16]
	mov	rax, qword ptr [rax + 16]
	mov	rax, qword ptr [rax]
	lea	rax, [rdi + rsi]
	pop	rbp
	ret
; └
; ┌ @ int.jl:87 within `<invalid>`
	nop	word ptr cs:[rax + rax]
; └

julia> code_native(+, Tuple{Int, Int}, dump_module=false, raw=true)
	.text
; ┌ @ int.jl:87 within `+`
	push	rbp
	mov	rbp, rsp
	mov	rax, qword ptr [r13 + 16]
	mov	rax, qword ptr [rax + 16]
	mov	rax, qword ptr [rax]
	lea	rax, [rdi + rsi]
	pop	rbp
	ret
; └
; ┌ @ int.jl:87 within `<invalid>`
	nop	word ptr cs:[rax + rax]

Julia, bitshifting a bitinteger x by more bits than is present in x causes x to be set to zero. Also, negative bitshifts are supported. This might semantically be more correct (and the former also matches LLVM's definition of bitshifts), but it does not correspond to either x86 or AArch64 behaviour. The result is that Julia's bitshifts are not optimised to a single instruction.
In constrast, in the C language, bitshifting by more than the bitwidth is UB, which allows the compiler to assume it never happens, and optimise the shift to a single instruction.

This commit adds the wrapping shift functions >>% >>>% and <<%, which overflows if the shift is too high or negative. The overflow behaviour is explicitly not stated, but the implemented behaviour matches the native behaviour of x86 and AArch64.

This commit requires JuliaSyntax support, which will be implemented in a parallel PR to JuliaSyntax.

## TODO
- [ ] Do we want this? I really want this in Julia, but maybe triage can make a decision here
- [ ] I need to test if these function compiles down to a single instruction on AArch64. If anyone has an ARM computer and can help me check it, that'd be grand
- [ ] JuliaSyntax.jl needs to be updated to support the parsing of `>>%` etc. See https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/408
- [ ] Maybe the flisp parser also needs to be updated? Let me know if so.
- [ ] Make sure this doesn't interfere with bootstrapping - move out of `int.jl` if necessary

```
for T1 in Base.BitInteger64_types
    for T2 in Base.BitInteger64_types
        for f in [foo, bar, qux]
            print(T1, " ", T2, " ", f)
            io = IOBuffer()
            code_native(io, f, (T1, T2); dump_module=false, debuginfo=:none, raw=true)
            s = collect(eachline(IOBuffer(String(take!(io)))))
            filter!(s) do i
                ss = lstrip(i)
                all(!startswith(ss, j) for j in [r"mov\s", r"pop\s", r"nop\s", r"ret\s?", r"push\s"])
            end
            for i in s
                println(i)
            end
        end
    end
end
```
It prints the emitted instructions for each type, filtering for the trivial ones like moving, pushing returning. Then I go through the list and make sure every combination only emits a shift.
The `all(!startswith(ss, j) for j in [r"mov\s", r"pop\s", r"nop\s", r"ret\s?", r"push\s"])` line may need some modification for AArch though. Specifically, check there are no jumps, conditional moves, calls or anything like that.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1883260307/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

In particular, I want the semantics to be:
* For an operator `f(a, b)`, where `f` is any of the three shift operators the valid values of `b` is `0:8*sizeof(a)-1`, of any `Base.BitInteger` type.
* Given a valid shift value, compute the same result as the ordinary shift operators as efficiently as possible
* Given an invalid shift value, we guarantee the function still returns, and still returns the correct type, but we make no guarantees about the returned value.
* In practice, the implementation might differ between different architectures, such that it always compile down to just a single instruction. AFAIK we can't write arch-specific Julia code ATM, but we can optimise it for x86 for now and then change it later. 

> Using the word overflowing as a jargony standin for "undefined behavior" seems not great.

I don't think "undefined behaviour" is a good term. People associate it with nasal demons and compilers doing terrible things like deleting whole functions, and your entire program being considered invalid such that anything goes. This is very much not the case with invalid bitshift values here. We guarantee the function returns in an orderly fashion, although the returned value is unspecified. I've changed the docstrings to be more precise, but I've avoided the term "undefined behaviour".

> How common is the operation you're exposing?

I'm not sure. I use it a lot in my own code, but I don't really have a sense of how common this is. If I'm atypical, it makes sense to preserve the `>>%` operators for something else. From my point of view, they're used more often than `bitrotate` for sure (these functions are even used in the current Julia implementation of `bitrotate`, through bit hacks). I suspect they're used a fair bit in high performance code - after all, that's one of the things Julia excel at, and that's when you want to go as close to the metal as possible.

>  There is no definition for an arbitrary Integer in either position. A generic fallback should be provided.

I disagree. This function is all about doing bit operations as efficiently as possible, by matching the CPUs native instructions. It makes no sense to provide a generic implementation, that would only mislead people into thinking there is an efficient implementation for their custom `Integer` type. If you have an unknown `Integer` type (or write completely generic code), there is no way you can do super efficient bit operations on it anyway, and you should just use the standard bitshift operators. Staying this close to the metal only makes sense if you know approximately what data you have.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1883463972/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Julia, bitshifting a bitinteger x by more bits than is present in x causes x to be set to zero. Also, negative bitshifts are supported. This might semantically be more correct (and the former also matches LLVM's definition of bitshifts), but it does not correspond to either x86 or AArch64 behaviour. The result is that Julia's bitshifts are not optimised to a single instruction.
In constrast, in the C language, bitshifting by more than the bitwidth is UB, which allows the compiler to assume it never happens, and optimise the shift to a single instruction.

This commit adds the wrapping shift functions >>% >>>% and <<%, which results in unspecified values if the shift is too high (>= the number of bits of the left operand) or negative. The resulting value in those case is explicitly not stated, but the implemented behaviour matches the native behaviour of x86 and AArch64.

This commit requires JuliaSyntax support, which will be implemented in a parallel PR to JuliaSyntax.

call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":23,"created_at":"2024-01-09T11:12:30Z","updated_at":"2024-01-10T08:37:00Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/52828","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"In Julia, bitshifting a `B`-bit integer `x` by B bits causes the result to be `zero(x)`. Also, negative bitshifts are supported, e.g. `x << -1`. This might semantically be sensible, but it does not correspond to either x86 or AArch64 behaviour. The result is that Julia's bitshifts are not optimised to a single instruction, which makes them unnecessarily slow in some performance sensitive contexts.
In constrast, in the C language, bitshifting by more than the bitwidth is undefined behaviour, which allows the compiler to assume it never happens, and optimise the shift to a single assembly instruction.

This commit adds the functions `unsafe_ashr`, `unsafe_lshr` and `unsafe_shl`. For each of these functions e.g. `unsafe_shl(x, n)`, if `n in 0:8*sizeof(x)-1`, it is equivalent to `x << n`. If `n` is not, then an arbitrary value may be returned, but we guarantee that
* The function returns with a value, and
* The value is of the same type as if `n` had been valid

I believe this behaviour will be sufficient to allow these functions to compile to maximally efficient code, without having some of the worst consequences of actual undefined behaviour in the C sense of the word.

The actual implementation will be whatever is most efficient on x86_64. If Julia ever gains the ability to write platform-specific code, then special implementations for other architectures could be added.

## TODO
- [ ] I need to test if these function compiles down to a single instruction on AArch64. If anyone has an ARM computer and can help me check it, that'd be grand
- [ ] Make sure this doesn't interfere with bootstrapping - move out of `int.jl` if necessary

## Decisions
- [ ] Do we want this?
- [ ] What should the names be? Presumably `unsafe_[something]` to match `unsafe_trunc`.
- [ ] Should a fallback definition be created `(::Integer, ::Integer)`?

Closes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50225","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52828/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52828/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1884404010","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828#issuecomment-1884404010","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52828","id":1884404010,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5wUbkq","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-10T08:36:59Z","updated_at":"2024-01-10T08:36:59Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Unfortunately, that implementation doesn't map exactly onto x86 shift instructions, instead it compiles to an `and` and a `shr` instruction. IIUC correctly, this is because there is no native 8-bit shift operations. So, to use 32-bit operations on 8-bit numbers with the code you wrote there, the CPU needs to make sure that bits 4 and 5 are zeroed out first. In contrast, the implementation in my PR compiles to a single shift instruction. It does have slightly different semantics when the shift is too high though:

Your code:
```julia
julia> 0x7f >>% 0x08
0x7f
```

My code:
```julia
julia> 0x7f >>% 0x08
0x00
```
Unfortunately, I think the edge cases around different bitsizes such as 8-bit integers are slightly different in AArch64 and x86, and it seems silly to me to go through the trouble of defining a fast, native bitshift which is not optimally fast. After all, if a user _really_ wants fast bitshifts, it's no good to emit an extra instruction. Conversely, if it isn't used in a hot loop, the normal shift operators are fine.

I struggle to see the usecase for having a kind of bitshift which is defined in terms of shifting with a modulo, and which isn't optimally fast. Where would that find use? I.e. what is the point of totally specifying the resulting value in cases of a too high bitshift?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1884404010/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
