call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":28,"created_at":"2024-01-09T11:12:30Z","updated_at":"2024-01-11T10:18:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/52828","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"In Julia, bitshifting a `B`-bit integer `x` by B bits causes the result to be `zero(x)`. Also, negative bitshifts are supported, e.g. `x << -1`. This might semantically be sensible, but it does not correspond to either x86 or AArch64 behaviour. The result is that Julia's bitshifts are not optimised to a single instruction, which makes them unnecessarily slow in some performance sensitive contexts.
In constrast, in the C language, bitshifting by more than the bitwidth is undefined behaviour, which allows the compiler to assume it never happens, and optimise the shift to a single assembly instruction.
The difference of one CPU instruction vs a handful may seem trivial, but in performance sensitive code this can really matter, e.g. https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30674 and https://github.com/attractivechaos/plb2/pull/48.

This commit adds the functions `unsafe_ashr`, `unsafe_lshr` and `unsafe_shl`. The goal of these functions are to compile to single shift instructions in order to be as fast as possible.

## Decisions
### 1. Semantics when the shift is too high
What happens in the CPU when you shift `x >> n`, where `x` is a `B` bit integer an `n >= B`? Let's call these "overflowing shifts". As far as I can tell, on x86, AArch and RISCV - so, basically all instruction sets that matter - only the lower 5 bits of `n` are used for 8-32 bit integers, and only the lower 6 bits for 64-bit integers.
Note that this implies that when `x` and `n` are 8-bit integers, masking `n` by `0x07` does __NOT__ correspond to the native shift instruction - it should be masked by `0x1f`. I'm not 100% certain about that - all the documentation I can find  simply assumes 32-bit operands.

So, what options do we have?

#### a) Completely undefined behaviour
Like C, we use LLVM's `assume` (or something similar) to simply assume that overflow doesn't happen. This produces fastest code, but it's probably too unsafe compared to the minor speed gain (~8% in a sample benchmark from Matt Bauman below). There is also a question about whether we even CAN implement this in Julia

#### b) Shift with `n % max(32, 8*sizeof(x)`
This allows us to use 32-bit shifts for 8-bit and 16-bit integers, which probably has around the same performance as the truly unsafe variant. The downside is that the behaviour is weirdly complex, matching the weird complexity of x86 bitshifts. A variation of this is to simply state that we promise the return type is the same as for non-overflow shifts, but we just say an arbitrary value will be returned.

#### c) Shift with `n % (8*sizeof(x))`
On x86, this produces optimal code for 64 and 32 bits, and is a little slower on 8 and 16 bit integers (Matt's GCD benchmark runs ~8.5% slower).

### 2. What should the name be for e.g. the equivalent of `>>`?
#### `>>%`
Pro: It's short, it looks like `>>`, and it's an infix operator, which makes it much more readable in complex bitshifting code

Con: It takes up valuable "ASCII real estate value", and the proposed semantics of `>>%` vs `>>` is different from `+%` vs `+` [as proposed by Keno](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50790), which might be confusing

#### `unsafe_ashr`
The `unsafe_` prefix is a nice way to warn that the resulting value may be arbitrary (if we go with that behaviour), similarly to `unsafe_trunc`. However, it may give misleading annotations of e.g. memory unsafety that other `unsafe_` functions can cause. It's also long and annoying to use in bit hacking compared with infix operators.

### 3. Should we have a fallback definition `(::Integer, ::Integer)`
Pro: It makes generic code possible with these operations, and it makes it less annoying for users who don't have to define their own implementations

Con: A processor-native bitshift only really makes sense for BitIntegers, and adding a generic function is semantically misleading

Closes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50225","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52828/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52828/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1886790939","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52828#issuecomment-1886790939","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52828","id":1886790939,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5wdiUb","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-11T10:18:09Z","updated_at":"2024-01-11T10:18:09Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"One more benchmark: Same as Matt's above, but with two differences:
* To see the effect of the reduction in instructions, I add `@inline` instead of `@noinline` to the gcd function
* I also test one more set of functions, `⪡` and `⪢` which displays the safe, but platform specific overflow behaviour I advocate. Results for Int64 and Int32 are the same. But for Int16 and Int8:
```
julia> A = rand(Int16, 100_000);

julia> @btime f(<<, >>, A);
  998.830 μs (1 allocation: 16 bytes)

julia> @btime f(<<ᵐ, >>ᵐ, A);
  942.925 μs (1 allocation: 16 bytes)

julia> @btime f(<<ᵃ, >>ᵃ, A);
  906.677 μs (1 allocation: 16 bytes)

julia> @btime f(⪡, ⪢, A);
  886.559 μs (1 allocation: 16 bytes)

julia> A = rand(Int8, 100_000);

julia> @btime f(<<, >>, A);
  1.007 ms (1 allocation: 16 bytes)

julia> @btime f(<<ᵐ, >>ᵐ, A);
  945.901 μs (1 allocation: 16 bytes)

julia> @btime f(<<ᵃ, >>ᵃ, A);
  909.542 μs (1 allocation: 16 bytes)

julia> @btime f(⪡, ⪢, A);
  887.090 μs (1 allocation: 16 bytes)
```
So, somehow even faster than the unsafe assume one, despite it being safe, and about 12% faster than the default bitshifts.
With the `@inline`, I see `⪡`, and `⪢` performing on par with the unsafe assume one - slightly slower on 8-bit ones, slightly faster on 16-bit ones.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1886790939/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```
Binbench
├── Manifest.toml
├── Project.toml
├── README.md
├── src
│  ├── 20231130.jl
│  └── main.jl
[ more files and folders ... ]
```
When installing packages into my project, I got:
```
ERROR: expected the file `src/Binbench.jl` to exist for package `Binbench` at `/home/jakni/Documents/binbench`
```
When adding `src/Binbench.jl`, I got the even more obscure:
```
WARNING: --output requested, but no modules defined during run
lld: error: cannot open /home/jakni/.julia/compiled/v1.10/Binbench/jl_HRxkzA: No such file or directory
  ◑ Binbench
```
This is not a package, it's just a scientific project with an associated Project.toml.
My issues are twofold:

1. Why does Julia insist this has to be formatted like a package? Why can't I just have a bunch of scripts in a directory with a Project file?
2. The error message is not great.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52974","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52974/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52974/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52974/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52974","id":2090124886,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM58lMZW","number":52974,"title":"`walkdir` fails to throw `IOError`, against docs","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-01-19T09:54:05Z","updated_at":"2024-01-19T09:54:05Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE:
```julia
julia> collect(walkdir("dsaasds"))
Tuple{String, Vector{String}, Vector{String}}[]
```

I believe this is a bug, per the docstring of `walkdir`, which promises:
>  If walkdir or stat encounters a IOError it will rethrow the error by default.

The bug happens due to the following code in `walkdir`. The context is that `walkdir` returns a `Channel`, in the code called `chnl`:
```julia
try
    f(p) # a readdir call
catch err
    isa(err, IOError) || rethrow()
    try
        onerror(err) # this is throw(err)
    catch err2
        close(chnl, err2)
    end
    return
end
```
So this happens:
* `f(p)` raises an `IOError`
* `onerror(err)` occurs in the inner try block, throwing the error again
* This is caught by the second `catch` block, closing the channel with this error. This is wrong, because the error is never thrown","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52974/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52974/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

```
julia> test(x::Vector{Tc}, λ::T) where {T <: Real, Tc <: Union{T, Complex{T}}} = T
test (generic function with 1 method)

Float64

 [2] top-level scope

You could have:
```julia
julia> my_eltype(x::Union{Int, Set{T}}) where T = x isa Int ? nothing : T

julia> my_eltype(1), my_eltype(Set(1))
(nothing, Int64)
```
But then you could change it a bit to
```julia
julia> my_eltype(x::Union{Int, Set{T}}) where T = T

julia> my_eltype(Set(1))
Int64

julia> my_eltype(1)
ERROR: UndefVarError: `T` not defined
```
I.e. `T` is defined when it's passed a set, but not if it's passed an int. It would be nice if Julia was able to throw an error already on method definition, but I don't know if it's possible to know T is not defined before the method is compiled with a specific type.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1900958092/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

reported on Discourse [here](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/union-splitting-not-working-in-julia-1-10/108710?u=petersimon), there is a regression shown by the following code:
```
julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> x = rand(10_000);

julia> function badsum(x)
               s = 0
               for t in x
                   s += t
               end
               return s
           end
badsum (generic function with 1 method)

julia> function goodsum(x)
           s = zero(eltype(x))
           for t in x
               s += t
           end
           return s
       end
goodsum (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @btime goodsum($x)
  8.667 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
5011.233699365655

julia> @btime badsum($x)
  17.400 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
5011.233699365655

julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.10.0
Commit 3120989f39 (2023-12-25 18:01 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Windows (x86_64-w64-mingw32)
  CPU: 8 × Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-9700 CPU @ 3.00GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-15.0.7 (ORCJIT, skylake)
  Threads: 1 on 8 virtual cores
Environment:
  JULIA_EDITOR = runemacs.exe
```
The same Discourse thread identifies the commits where the regression is introduced: 
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/commit/4200aa9471bbe3af448931eb2b35fcfee0550e24

and the commit where it was later fixed for nightly:
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/commit/8e4221f676cc0cc242b93389b2f65084931e581c

To me it looks like the issue is that it's loop peeling that goes wrong in 1.10.
I.e. in earlier versions, Julia will
* Begin the loop with `s::Int`
* Peel the first iteration of the loop, computing `s::Float64 = s + first(x)`
* Since `s::Float64`, the loop is now a simple float-float addition

Whereas in 1.10, the peeling doesn't work and so it does
* Begin with `s::Int`
* For every iteration, it needs to promote `s::Union{Int, Float64}` and `x[i]::Float64`. improves speed and reduces allocation for small Dicts.
```
#before
julia> @btime Dict{Int,Int}()
  46.815 ns (4 allocations: 448 bytes)

#after
julia> @btime Dict{Int, Int}()
  9.429 ns (1 allocation: 80 bytes)
```
The initialization to 16 before made sense when `Dict` was `Vector` backed since `Vectors` were slow to instantiate, but Memory is much faster).

sets"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-01-22T12:59:18Z","updated_at":"2024-01-23T06:12:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```julia
julia> Vector{Union{}}(undef, 1)
1-element Vector{Union{}}:
 #undef
```

Two issues:

1. an array of type `<:AbstractArray{Union{}}` must always be empty (because `Union{}` never has instances). So why is the object being `show`-n as "`1-element Vector{Union{}}`"?

2. Given that `<:AbstractArray{Union{}}` can never have elements (or, more precisely I guess, `iterate` will either return `nothing` or throw), trying to construct such an array with a nonzero length should throw.

The example above is by @tpapp in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385#issuecomment-1903922191

 """
     <:(T1, T2)
 
-Subtype operator: returns `true` if and only if all values of type `T1` are
+Subtype predicate: returns `true` if and only if all values of type `T1` are
 also of type `T2`.
 
+Julia is sometimes not able to prove a subtyping relation that would ideally
+hold between two types. When that happens, `<:` returns `false`.
+
+The `<:` predicate is expected (modulo bugs) to behave as a partial order. itself"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-01-30T15:07:30Z","updated_at":"2024-01-30T17:32:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It's my understanding that type unions should not be callable, i.e., they shouldn't have constructors, i.e., trying to call such a constructor should throw. The `Union` doc string says something that might be interpreted in such a way:

> Like other abstract types, it cannot be instantiated, even if all of its arguments are non abstract.

Would it be good to make these throw, then:

```julia-repl
julia> Union{Int8,Int}(4)
4

julia> Union{Tuple{},Tuple{Int}}(())
()

julia> Union{Tuple{},Tuple{Int}}((4,))
(4,)
```

Otherwise (if `Union` constructors are desirable), I guess that the documentation should mention that newly defined types should support `Union` constructors somehow.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53120/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53120/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1917551409","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53120#issuecomment-1917551409","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53120","id":1917551409,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5yS4Mx","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-30T17:32:43Z","updated_at":"2024-01-30T17:32:43Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"There are also abstract constructors several places in Julia. the consistency when trying to construct a `Union` type from a `Number` value.

Before:

```julia
Union{Int8,Int}(4)    # returns `4`

```

After:

```julia
Union{Int8,Int}(4)    # returns `4`
Union{String,Int}(4)  # returns `4`
```

```julia
julia> using AllocCheck

julia> @check_allocs function foobars(d::Dict{Tuple{Float64, Float64}, Int})
           peeled = Iterators.peel(keys(d))
           isnothing(peeled) && return nothing
           (recall, precision), rest = peeled
           (max_recall, max_precision, max_f1) = (recall, precision, recall + precision)
           for (recall, precision) in rest
               this_f1 = recall + precision
               if this_f1 > max_f1
                   (max_f1, max_recall, max_precision) = (recall, precision, this_f1)
               end
           end
           (max_recall, max_precision)
       end

+    end

Channels","user":{"login":"tamasgal","id":1730350,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3MzAzNTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1730350?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tamasgal","html_url":"https://github.com/tamasgal","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tamasgal/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tamasgal/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tamasgal/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tamasgal/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tamasgal/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tamasgal/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tamasgal/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tamasgal/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tamasgal/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-12-10T00:17:24Z","updated_at":"2023-12-10T09:52:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/52470","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52470","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52470.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52470.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Currently the only way to check if a `Channel` is full by accessing its fields `.data` and `.sz_max` which is neither a good  practice, nor documented.

Checking if a (buffered) channel is full is mandatory when somebody wants to discard the data to avoid e.g. memory pile-up or redirect it to another channel (load-balancing).

This PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)` which returns `true` if the number of available items is equal (or exceeds -- still not sure if that case can happen due to race conditions) the size of the buffered channel.

A somewhat similar problem is also addressed in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/41966 with the implementation of `tryput!`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52470/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52470/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1848913652","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52470#issuecomment-1848913652","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52470","id":1848913652,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5uNC70","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-12-10T09:52:03Z","updated_at":"2023-12-10T09:52:03Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Do we have a supported way to check if a channel is buffered? Currently, `isbuffered` is unexported.
I think in the absence of such a function, having the API of this function here return `false` when the channel is unbuffered is not a good idea, because a user has no way of knowing if `isfull` returns true because there is still room in the channel, or because it's unbuffered.

Another issue is - suppose a user calls `isfull` on a buffered channel, and it returns `false`. Can the user then put values into it? Maybe not! If another thread has filled it immediately after the `isfull` call, the call will be misleading. A better way may be something similar to `tryput!` - the user tries to put the value in, and gets told if the channel is full, or closed, or the value was successfully put in.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1848913652/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

+    # Check if elements in the vector are of the right type
+    if !(all(isequal(eltype(delim), UInt8) || issubclass(eltype(delim), AbstractChar) for d in delim))
+        throw(ArgumentError("readuntil: elements in the vector must be of type UInt8 or AbstractChar"))

loading"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":33,"created_at":"2023-11-09T14:42:27Z","updated_at":"2023-12-26T17:19:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/52103","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52103","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52103.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52103.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This aims to bring similar functionality to Julia as the `-m` flag for Python which exists to directly run some function in a package and being able to pass arguments to that function.

While in Python, `python -m package args` runs the file `<package>.__main__.py`, the equivalent Julia command (`julia -m Package args`) instead runs `<Package>.main(args)`. The package is assumed to be installed in the environment `julia` is run in.

An example usage could be:

Add the package:
```julia
(@v1.11) pkg> add https://github.com/KristofferC/Rot13.jl
     Cloning git-repo `https://github.com/KristofferC/Rot13.jl`
    Updating git-repo `https://github.com/KristofferC/Rot13.jl`
   Resolving package versions...
    Updating `~/.julia/environments/v1.11/Project.toml`
  [43ef800a] + Rot13 v0.1.0 `https://github.com/KristofferC/Rot13.jl#master`
    Updating `~/.julia/environments/v1.11/Manifest.toml`
  [43ef800a] + Rot13 v0.1.0 `https://github.com/KristofferC/Rot13.jl#master`
```

And then it can be run (since it has a `main` function) via:

```
❯ ./julia/julia -m Rot13 "encrypt this for me" "and this as well"
rapelcg guvf sbe zr
naq guvf nf jryy
```

I'm not sure if `-m/--module` is the best choice but perhaps the association to Python makes it worth it.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52103/reactions","total_count":15,"+1":5,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":9,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52103/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1869674944","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52103#issuecomment-1869674944","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52103","id":1869674944,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5vcPnA","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-12-26T17:19:24Z","updated_at":"2023-12-26T17:19:24Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I'm not convinced this provide any real benefit. If I understand correctly, `julia -m MyPackage` is equivalent to `julia -e 'using MyPackage: main; main()'`. Alternatively, I could just make a two-line script that loads the package and executes it. I don't see any real value in a few feature just to save that handful of keystrokes.

Indeed, this PR may do more harm than good, because it seems to me to solve a non-problem, while not addressing the current problems with executing Julia code. I believe the more pressing gotchas with running Julia scripts from command-line are:
* Having to create a global environment and run the code in that environment
* Disabling stacked environments to make sure dependencies from higher environment aren't relied on when testing the script
* Disabling `startup.jl` which may interfere with the loaded environment
* The various issues stemming from our JIT model, including latency and the absence of a single executable file (addressing these is way outside the scope of this PR, I know)

This is not to disrepect the work you're doing, Kristoffer, to make Julia more easy to execute. But as this PR doesn't address any of the issues above, I worry that it may in effect add a feature which I would never recommend anyone to use, and which will clutter the "solution space" and make it harder to navigate for Julia newbies.

For comparison, Comonicon.jl creates an executable Julia file with the following content, which does address all my concerns above:
```julia
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#=
JULIA_PROJECT=/home/jakob/.julia/scratchspaces/b91f9d24-e2a8-4b03-b25e-993ad86b9958/env \
exec /home/jakob/.julia/juliaup/julia-1.10.0-beta3+0.x64.linux.gnu/bin/julia \
    --startup-file=no \
    --color=yes \
    --compile=yes \
    --optimize=2 \
    -- "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" "$@"
=#

# generated by Comonicon for the CLI Application binbench
using MyPackage
exit(MyPackage.command_main())
```

Perhaps the `-m` flag in this PR could include the above changes (and thus this PR would also close #42205)?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1869674944/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Description
Add a method to `parse`: `parse(::Type{<:Type}, ::AbstractString)`, which parses a string into a julia type object.

This replaces the only alternative previously available, which was to `eval(Meta.parse(str))`.

This is beneficial for two major reasons:
1. It's much faster to directly construct the type object than to call `eval()`, which is a very general mechanism.
    - Around 5x-15x faster based on microbenchmarks.
3. It's much _safer_ for application code to construct a type than to call eval.
    - If the type string comes from external data, eval can expose the application to an injection attack. For example, given the string `s = "precompile(Tuple{typeof(+), dump_all_your_secrets()})"`, `eval(Meta.parse(s))` would _invoke unwanted code_.

Some microbenchmark results:
```julia
julia> VERSION
v"1.11.0-DEV.1167"

julia> @btime eval(Meta.parse($("Main.Main.Base.Checked.Vector{Dict{Int, Base.Float64}}")))
  130.333 μs (219 allocations: 10.06 KiB)
Vector{Dict{Int64, Float64}} (alias for Array{Dict{Int64, Float64}, 1})

julia> @btime parse($(Type), $("Main.Main.Base.Checked.Vector{Dict{Int, Base.Float64}}"))
  9.166 μs (143 allocations: 7.38 KiB)
Vector{Dict{Int64, Float64}} (alias for Array{Dict{Int64, Float64}, 1})
```

## Notes:

#### Is this really parsing?

This capability has been discussed / requested by members of the community in the past. The answer has always been to parse+eval. For example in this thread, the OP was told to eval the string: https://discourse.julialang.org/t/parse-string-to-datatype/7118/8.

In that thread, the argument is made that this isn't "parsing," since Type objects are not literals from the parser's perspective. This really is more like parse+eval. In which case, I'm happy to use a different name for this if people feel strongly about it. But to me, it _feels like_ parsing, so `parse(Type, str)` does feel like the right API for this.

I'm open to suggestions on that!

## Remaining Work

I first wrote this with only `DataType`s in mind. You can see the version that parses _only_ DataTypes in the first commit, 28ee0f3e963e22752e6f5d1d7be8a779fbfdacac.  But then I realized that we should also support UnionAlls, which requires a bit more work. I'm still working through how best to do that... I could probably use some help with this, TBH.


- [ ] The current implementation is still not correct for all UnionAll types:
    - [ ] `Vector{<:Number}`
    - [ ] `Main.Vector{S} where {Int<:T<:Number, S<:T}`
    - [x] `typeof(+)`
- [ ] The current implementation for UnionAlls support is awkward, and an after-thought, and could be _much_ improved.
- [ ] I need to implement `tryparse`, and use that, to allow parse failures without throwing an exception.
- [ ] more tests
- [ ] Is it okay that the code is currently relying on `Meta.parse` and then walking the parsed AST?
    - That's where the bulk of the CPU time goes now.. Would it be preferential to write custom parsing code straight from the string to the Types?
    - It doesn't really make sense to me to write custom code for this - what we need to do is a strict subset of what Meta.parse needs to do, and I think it's probably best to share that work as much as possible. I think that to support UnionAlls, we need the later parts of the parse-tree (e.g. the where-clause) before we can interpret the earlier parts anyway.
    - _Maybe_ we could directly call into a subset of `Meta.parse`'s implementation though?
- [ ] News
- [ ] Docs: Should this have a separate docstring, or just extend the main top-level one?
